content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 20, 2015

Amy W. Estrada
Counsel
Atkinson, Andelson, Loya, Ruud & Romo
16870 West Berardo Drive, Suite 330
San Diego, CA 92127-1671

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-15-232

Dear Ms. Estrada:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Alpine Union School District
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that
we do not advise on any other area of law, including the School District's nepotism policies. We are
also not a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) We do not
advise on past conduct under either the Act or Section 1090. The discussion below applies only to
future events.

For advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090,
the advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding brought against any individual other than the
requestor." (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

Because your request is general in nature, we are treating it as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does either the Act or Section 1090 limit a school board member's participation in future
decisions or contracts when his spouse is a classified employee of the District?

CONCLUSION

Both the Act and Section 1090 may limit a board member from participating in certain
decisions, as discussed below.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-15-232
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are counsel for the Alpine Union School District. In December 2012, Joseph Perricone
took an elected seat on the district's Board of Trustees. The Boardmember's spouse is a long-time
district employee (last promoted in September 2012 and serves as the district's Human Resources
Specialist). The Board of Trustees makes all decisions regarding approving and ratifying salaries
for district employees. As a Board Member, Mr. Perricone could make future employment
decisions that could affect his wife.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 concerns financial
interests, other than remote or minimal ones, that prevent public officials from exercising
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Sigal v.
Tafi (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at actual impropriety,
but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980)
103Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a
financial interest. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) The result of a contract that is void based on a Section 1090 violation is that the party that
contracted with the public entity must disgorge any profit that resulted from the contract and the
public entity keeps the benefit of the contract. (See Carson Redevelopment Agency v. Padilla
(2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323.)

Step One-Three: Is there a public officer who is subject to Section 1090 participating
in contract decisions?

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Mr. Perricone, as a Board Member, is a public officer subject to Section 1090. As a Board
Member, his participation is presumed in all contracts considered by the Board while he is a
member. Section 1090 therefore prohibits any decisions once he is on the Board in which he has a
financial interest.

Step Four: Does Mr. Perricone have a financial interest in the contract?

Under Section 1090, officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they
might profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Although Section 1090 does
not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state

File No. I-15-232
Page No. 3

that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Valterga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).)

Mr. Perricone has a financial interest in his spouse's salary and in all contracts affecting his spouse's employment. (Glover Advice Letter, A-14-138; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 175 (2001); Sodergren Advice Letter, A-14-202.) Thus, Section 1090 bars the Board from making any decisions that affects his wife's employment contract unless an exception applies.

Step Five: Does an exception apply?

There are several statutory exceptions to the prohibition in Section 1090. The remote exceptions in Section 1091 apply only to the members of a body or board, and do not apply to employees. The non-interest exception to Section 1090 states that one spouse has a non-interest in the other's employment if the employment has existed for at least one year prior to his or her election or appointment to office. (Section 1091.5(a)(6).)

In Thorpe v. Long Beach Community College District (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655, one spouse worked for the district for several years before her husband was elected to the district board. After the husband took the seat on the district board, the court found that his wife could maintain her current position, but the terms of the employment could not change. Thus, there could be no promotion or similar change in status while he was on the Board. Similarly, the Attorney General issued an opinion stating that the spouse of a school board member could have his or her teaching contract annually renewed so long as the spouse was not promoted or appointed to a new position. (69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 255 (1986).) But, the board of trustees of a community college district may not approve a selective reclassification of a classified employee's position, if the employee's spouse is a member of the board of trustees and the reclassification makes the employee eligible for an increase in salary. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 175 (2001).)

The cases above are clear that if Mrs. Perricone is eligible for a change in the terms of her employment while Mr. Perricone is on the Board of Trustees, the ensuing contract could violate Section 1090, but the non-interest exception for spousal employment could apply.

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or in any way using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know that he or she has a disqualifying conflict of interest. Under Section 87103, a public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of his or her immediate family.

File No. 1-15-232
Page No. 4

A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c).) Regarding income, a public official's income, for purposes of the Act, also includes his
or her community property interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).)

Clearly, an employer is a source of income in the form of salary and benefits. As a member
of the Board of Trustees, Mr. Perricone has a community property interest in any source of
Mrs. Perricone's income. (Section 82030.) Where the employer is a local governmental agency,
however, there is generally no economic interest because of the "government salary" exception. The
Act's definition of income expressly excludes "salary and reimbursement for expenses and per diem
received from a . . . local . . . government agency . . .." (Section 82030(b)(2).) This exception also
covers employment-related benefits, such as pensions. Thus, unless the Perricones receive income
from the district that is not covered by the "governmental salary" exception, the district does not
qualify as a source of income.

Notwithstanding the "governmental salary" exception, a conflict of interest may still exist in
decisions on Mrs. Perricone's hiring, firing, promotion, demotion or discipline, or setting a salary
that is different from salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification or position.
These decisions will affect Mr. Perricone's personal finances. Other decisions that affect all
employees in the same job classification in the same manner will not result in disqualification.
(Campbell Advice Letter, No. A-94-002.)

When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the
discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as
well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself,
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105;
Regulation 18707.) School board members are not explicitly listed in Section 87200, but it does
apply to "other public officials who manage public investments." If the school board manages
public investments this provision applies to Mr. Perricone.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.question:
Does either the Act or Section 1090 limit a school board member's participation in future 
decisions or contracts when his spouse is a classified employee of the District?

decisions, as discussed below.

sections.analysis:
Section J 090

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 concerns financial 
interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at actual impropriety, 
but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. ( City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey ( 1980) 
103Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a 
financial interest. (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates

regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) The result of a contract that is void based on a Section l 090 violation is that the party that 
contracted with the public entity must disgorge any profit that resulted from the contract and the

(2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1323.)

member. Section l 090 therefore prohibits any decisions once he is on the Board in which he has a 
financial interest. 
Step Four: Does Mr. Perricone have a financial interest in the contract? 
Under Section 1090, officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they

not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state  
that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, 
or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Val/erga (1977) 67 
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 
161-162 (2001).) 
Mr. Perricone has a financial interest in his spouse's salary and in all contracts affecting his

There are several statutory exceptions to the prohibition in Section 1090. The remote

to employees. The non-interest exception to Section 1090 states that one spouse has a non-interest

spouse worked for the district for several years before her husband was elected to the district board.

The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or in any
